sections.heading: Early career
sections.content:
Lü Bu was born at Jiuyuan, Wuyuan Prefecture, Bing Province (modern day Baotou, Jiuyuan of Inner Mongolia). Lü Bu was famous in Bing Province for his horsemanship and archery skills. The earliest known recording of his military career began at Bing Province under Ding Yuan, who quickly favored him after he had enlisted. He began as a Chief Registrar under Ding Yuan.

Lü Bu followed his lord to Luoyang as Ding Yuan colluded with He Jin to deal with the eunuchs who reigned after Emperor Ling's death. To intimidate pro-eunuch officials, especially He Jin's sister, Empress Dowager He, Ding Yuan set fire to Mengjin Port near Luoyang. Ding Yuan was entitled Bearer of the Gilded Mace for his deeds. Another warlord in capital, Dong Zhuo, hoping to seize power, tempted Lü Bu with offers of profit, hoping he would assassinate his lord. Lü Bu killed Ding Yuan and delivered his head to Dong Zhuo. Dong Zhuo, pleased with Lü Bu's actions, appointed him a Captain of the Cavalry.

In 190, Dong Zhuo sent Lü Bu to rob the tombs of emperors and ministers of past dynasties and collect the treasures inside. Dong Zhuo then burned Luoyang and abducted the local population to Chang'an. Xu Rong decisively defeated Cao Cao and Bao Xin, who who were pursuing from Yan Province. Xu Rong subsequently plundered Yu Province, defeating both Sun Jian and Li Min, torturing and killing the captured Li Min. Sun Jian managed to survive and gather the remaining troops. Lü Bu first marched to battle Dong Zhuo's oppressors in 191, he was a part of the subjugation army to intercept Sun Jian's forces at Yangren. Lü Bu is not known to have contributed much for the battle, instead choosing to bicker with the equally arrogant and foul natured Hu Zhen. Since they were members of the same cavalry unit, Lü Bu made his grudge apparent by lying about Hu Zhen's orders. The troops were miserably confused and disorganized and caused Hu Zhen to suffer defeat. Sun Jian then attacked Luoyang and defeated Lü Bu, who remained there. However, Yuan Shao, dissatisfied with Yuan Shu's continued expansion, sent Zhou Yu (Renming) to attack Yu Province. Sun Jian then abandoned the fight with Dong Zhuo, retreated, and turned to fight Yuan Shao.

Since Lü Bu was infamous for his prowess, Dong Zhuo tried to keep him beside him to deter his many hated rivals in Chang'an. His new master loved him like a son, they swore to become adopted father and son. Lü Bu eventually was given the titles Commander of the Palace Guards and Secondary Marquis of Du.

sections.heading: Mutiny with Wang Yun
sections.content:
Wang Yun, who had been exhausted by Dong Zhuo's iron grip around this time, conspired to assassinate his lord. He wanted other retainers who shared his beliefs to join his uprising. Somehow, he convinced Lü Bu to join his cause. Lü Bu expressed concern over slaying his adoptive father might be a very immoral act. Wang Yun convinced Lü Bu that Dong Zhuo had not literally adopted him, as evidenced by the fact that he had retained his surname. Furthermore, Dong Zhuo's violent treatment of Lü Bu was not the same as treating a son. Wang Yun said, "Right now, you should worry more about whether you will be killed than about the (meaningless) father-son relationship."

There are three argued reasons why Lü Bu decided to join the plot against his adoptive father. One of them involves an incident in which Dong Zhuo was angry with Lü Bu, unsheathed a short sword, and hurled it at him. Lü Bu was barely able to dodge the thrown weapon in time. The other concerns his secret trysts with one of Dong Zhuo's servant maids. He remained in constant anxiety if and when the other palace attendants learned of their relationship for he feared suffering Dong Zhuo's wrath. The third reason is thought to be fueled by jealousy for other retainers' promotions or positive treatment they received from Dong Zhuo. In either scenario, it's generally accepted that Wang Yun had used Lü Bu's anxieties to sway him for his coup d'état.

In April 192, Lü Bu and Li Su waited in secret at Endless (Weiyang 未央) Palace. Dong Zhuo wanted to visit the palace to celebrate Emperor Xian's recovery from illness and was lured there by Wang Yun earlier the same morning. Once Dong Zhuo had entered the palace, Li Su and his accomplices emerged to strike him down. Dong Zhuo was stabbed and fell from his carriage. However, Dong Zhuo had worn armor under his clothes and was only slightly wounded. Dong Zhuo cried out, "Where is Lü Bu?" Lü Bu emerged at this time. He answered, "I have an imperial edict to punish the traitorous minister", before he killed Dong Zhuo with his own blade. At Lü Bu's insistence, Dong Zhuo's relatives in Chang'an were massacred as well.

After the Dong family were slaughtered in Chang'an, Wang Yun claimed the capital. Lü Bu was given the title General of Summoned Might, Lü Bu was granted the same rank as the Three Excellencies and discussed state affairs with them, and was promoted to (County) Marquis of Wen. Despite his celebrated services, Lü Bu was loosely trusted by his comrades in Chang'an. Wang Yun had originally considered Lü Bu as a last resort to remove Dong Zhuo and many didn't approve of his attitude. Lü Bu made two suggestions to Wang Yun that might later be considered correct, namely, pardoning Li Jue and other remnants of Dong Zhuo who held the army, and rewarding the soldiers with the property looted by Dong Zhuo, but both were rejected by Wang Yun.

Approximately one or two months later, Chang'an was surrounded by Dong Zhuo's former retainers who sought to avenge their lord's death. According to Wang Can's Record of Heroes (Yingxiong Ji), Lü Bu confronted Guo Si in battle. Lü Bu opened the city gates and said to Guo Si, "Have the soldiers withdraw. Let us fight alone." Lü Bu wounded Guo Si with his spear, and Guo Si retreated as promised. If the incident is true, then it would be one of the rare instances in history in which a military conflict was ended by a duel between generals. Although Lü Bu led troops to oppose them, the 100,000 troops overpowered Chang'an's defenses in ten days. Before the castle had completely fallen, Lü Bu gathered several hundred horsemen, attached Dong Zhuo's head to his horse, and rode out of the castle to flee. Whilst his horse circled around Chang'an's gates, he implored Wang Yun to escape as well. Wang Yun refused the request for he didn't want to turn his back on those remaining and was prepared to face his punishment.

When Chang'an fell, Lü Bu and his men fled southward through Guanzhong's Wu Pass. He was able to keep his three noble titles for a mere sixty days.

sections.heading: Establishing Independence
sections.content:
Believing he had assassinated Dong Zhuo, and considering Yuan Shu considered him a benefactor, Lü Bu initially sought refuge with Yuan Shu in Nanyang. Yuan Shu treated him well, but later, unable to tolerate Lü Bu's predatory behavior, Lü Bu fled Yuan Shu and traveled to Ji Province to seek refuge with Yuan Shao. Yuan Shao temporarily housed Lü Bu's forces and used the general to fight his current adversary at the moment, Zhang Yan and his Black Mountain rebels. The rebels were armed in the hundred thousands and severely outnumbered Lü Bu's forces at Changshan. Lü Bu led several dozen horsemen and rode with his retainers, Wei Yue and Cheng Lian, to oppose Zhang Yan. They clashed with their foe three or four times and, ten days of fighting later, Zhang Yan's army suffered defeat.

Lü Bu's stay with Yuan Shao was a weary and short one. Yuan Shao had planned to keep using Lu Bu's abilities for his expeditionary forces, but he was disturbed by Lu Bu's willingness to pillage for supplies. There have been also argued accounts that Yuan Shao disapproved his troops accepting Lu Bu's thievery. His visitor's request for additional troops under his command therefore disgusted Yuan Shao. Once he learned of Yuan Shao's ire, Lu Bu mutually shared these feelings and sought to desert Yuan Shao. Fearing that Lü  Bu would strike against him, Yuan Shao planned to have the general assassinated. Lü Bu learned of the plot from his retainers and escaped before it succeeded. The Record of Heroes states that Yuan Shao sent 30 armored "bodyguards" to escort Lü Bu to Luoyang to take up his post as Inspector of the Imperial Guards. Lü Bu arranged for musicians to play in his tent and pretended to sleep there, while secretly slipping away. During the night, Yuan Shao's assassins hacked at Lü Bu's bed, believing they had killed him. Only in daylight did they discover they had been deceived. The next day, Yuan Shao continued to send new assassins, but they did not dare to approach Lü Bu, so Lü Bu successfully escaped.

During his time spent with Yuan Shao, Lü Bu had befriended Zhang Miao. He originally wanted to go to Zhang Miao, they initially got along well, even swearing an alliance. Zhang Miao had previously criticized Yuan Shao for abusing his power, and Yuan Shao ordered Cao Cao to execute Zhang Miao, but Cao Cao refused. Yuan Shao was furious when he learned of Zhang Miao's relationship with Lü Bu.

To avoid troubling Zhang Miao, Lü Bu eventually found another home at Henei with Zhang Yang. At the time, Li Jue and his men were desperately seeking Lü Bu. Zhang Yang and his men initially planned to kill Lü Bu and collect the bounty. Lü Bu persuaded Zhang Yang, saying, "Sir, you and I are from the same hometown. Your will gain less by killing me. It would be better to hand me over alive. Guo Si and Li Jue will surely reward you handsomely." Zhang Yang then changed his mind, pretending to agree to Li Jue's instructions while actually protecting Lü Bu. Convinced that they could not capture Lü Bu from Zhang Yang, Li Jue appointed Lü Bu as the prefect of Yingchuan, hoping that he would cause more trouble for their enemies.

After experiencing a falling out with Cao Cao and Yuan Shao, Zhang Miao conspired with Chen Gong to oppose Cao Cao. Both instigators then sought to make Lü Bu their figurehead lord. In 194, as they rebelled against Cao Cao's army at Yanzhou, Zhang Miao granted Lü Bu's entry into the thick of their conflict at Yan Province. Lü Bu and his forces successfully took Puyang away from Xiahou Dun during Cao Cao's absence. Lü Bu's forces tried to expand their influence deeper into Cao Cao's territory, but they were ultimately foiled by Xun Yu and Cheng Yu. When Cao Cao returned to Yan Province, he gathered troops to attack Lü Bu at Puyang. The following battle resulted in a disastrous defeat for Cao Cao who escaped due to the help of his capable vassals. Lü Bu was in a position to overwhelm Cao Cao, but the one hundred plus days of famine and the storm of locusts destroying crops and troop supplies stayed his hand. Lu Bu's forces eventually stationed themselves in Shanyang, which allowed Cao Cao's retreat.

Within two years, Cao Cao regained his power and gradually eliminated Lü Bu's followers. In the Book of the Later Han, published during the Southern Dynasties period, it is claimed that Yuan Shao mourned Cao Cao's defeat at the hands of Yuan Shao's enemy, Lü Bu, and provided Cao Cao with few thousands of reinforcements. Later, in his manifesto insulting Cao Cao, Yuan Shao attributed the credit of defeating Lü Bu to himself. Ultimately, Lü Bu's only remaining stronghold was Yongqiu, held by Zhang Chao, Zhang Miao's brother. Zhang Miao sought aid from Yuan Shu but was killed by mutinous soldiers, while Zhang Chao was defeated by Cao Cao and his entire clan executed. Defeated by Cao Cao at Juye, Lü Bu fled east to join Liu Bei, who had recently seized control of Xu Province from the deceased Tao Qian.

According to Record of Heroes, Lü Bu tried to get close to Liu Bei, saying, "Both you and I are from the frontier. I witnessed the uprising in Guandong, ostensibly to kill Dong Zhuo. I killed Dong Zhuo and fled east, but the generals in Guandong refused to take me in and instead tried to murder me." Lü Bu made Liu Bei sit on his woman's bed, had her pay her respects, and treated Liu Bei to wine and dinner, calling Liu Bei his younger brother. Liu Bei was reportedly annoyed, but he held his temper and sent Lü Bu to garrison at Xiaopei, west of his territory.

About a month later, Yuan Shu attacked Xu Province, and Liu Bei went to oppose him, leaving Zhang Fei to garrison Xiapi. Yuan Shu wrote a humble letter, saying that Lü Bu had helped defeat his three main enemies, Dong Zhuo, Cao Cao, and Liu Bei*, and hoped that Lü Bu would continue to help him attack Liu Bei's base in Xiapi, and he would send a large amount of supplies in return. Zhang Fei had a bad relationship with Cao Bao, a remnant of Tao Qian's faction, and he killed Cao Bao. Cao Bao's men colluded with Lü Bu, and Lü Bu rushed to Xiapi overnight. Cao Bao's men opened the city gates, allowing Lü Bu to scale the city walls. Using a fire attack, his men defeated Zhang Fei. Lü Bu captured Liu Bei's family, supplies, and the families of his officers.

Liu Bei, currently locked in a stalemate with Yuan Shu, learned of Lü Bu's mutiny and hurried back to Xiapi. However, his army collapsed upon reaching Xiapi. Liu Bei gathered his defeated troops and marched east to capture Guangling, attempting another battle with Yuan Shu, but was defeated. With his army depleted of supplies and forced to resort to cannibalism, Liu Bei was forced to surrender to Lü Bu, who had betrayed him.

Lü Bu promised to reinstate Liu Bei as governor, return Liu Bei's family, and treat him with the proper courtesy of a governor, declaring that they could unite to fight Yuan Shu. Convinced that Lü Bu would not be so kind, Liu Bei felt frightened and requested to station his troops in Xiaopei. Then Lü Bu proclaimed himself the Governor of Xu Province. Around this time, Yuan Shu ordered Ji Ling and others to assemble an army of 30,000 to attack Xiapi. Lü Bu's generals gloated over this, suggesting they could use Yuan Shu to kill Liu Bei. However, Lü Bu believed that if Yuan Shu eliminated Liu Bei and then joined forces with Zang Ba and others in Tai Mountains, he would be completely surrounded. Lü Bu also resented Yuan Shu for not fulfilling the bounty he had promised for attacking Liu Bei. He decided to intercept the invaders with 1,000 men and 200 horsemen.

As Lü Bu's troops reinforced Xiaopei, Ji Ling and others did not dare to act rashly. Lü Bu invited them to a feast and said to Yuan Shu's retainers, "Xuande is [my]* younger brother. Gentleman, I am aware that my brother has caused you unneeded hardship so I came to help. It is not in my nature to perpetuate conflict. Nay, I prefer to stop disputes."

He then ordered one of his soldiers to position a halberd upright at Xiaopei's gate. As he drew an arrow back from a bow given to him, Lü Bu addressed them again, "Gentleman, please observe if you would my shot at that halberd's wooden end. If my arrow hits it, then I ask that both armies withdraw and disband their troops. If I am to fail, then your troops may do as you please." With the same arrow, Lü Bu hit the halberd exactly at the spot mentioned in his barter. Ji Ling and company were astonished and exclaimed, "You are the very light of Heaven!" On the same day, both sides conversed a while longer and obeyed Lü Bu.

However, the relationship between Liu Bei and Lü Bu did not improve substantially. Upon learning that Liu Bei had mobilized a large army in Xiaopei, Lü Bu immediately sent troops to defeat Liu Bei, forcing him to surrender to Cao Cao.

The Record of Heroes claims that Emperor Xian asked for Lü Bu to receive his company when he was in Hedong. At the time, Lü Bu's supplies were low so the offer was respectfully refused. As Emperor Xian sent another messenger to reach Lü Bu, he included the appointed titles General who Pacifies the East and Pingtao Marquis in his letter. However, as the messenger left for Lü Bu, the letter was lost at Shanyang.

*This description is very strange. It is not known whether there were some unrecorded minor conflicts between Lu Bu and Liu Bei before he was expelled from Yan Province.

*Throughout the exchange, Lü Bu refers to himself in third person by full name. Literally, this usage is a humble tone, but it may actually be pressure.

sections.heading: Swinging between Han and Zhong
sections.content:
In June 196, Lü Bu's general Hao Meng launched a mutiny and attacked Lü Bu's office in Xiapi. Lü Bu was forced to escape naked from a toilet and seek refuge with Gao Shun. Cao Xing, a subordinate of Hao Meng, disapproved of the rebellion and attacked Hao Meng, allowing Gao Shun to suppress the rebellion and kill Hao Meng. Cao Xing accused Chen Gong of colluding with Yuan Shu to assassinate Lü Bu. However, Lü Bu chose to conceal the incident and instead punished Gao Shun by stripping him of his military power and giving it to Wei Xu, retaining him only in battle.

In 197, Yuan Shu proclaimed himself emperor in Huainan and named his empire Zhong. Yuan Shu desired to establish a solid alliance with Lü Bu. Yuan Shu sent Han Yin as his messenger and sought to create their alliance by marrying Yuan Shu's son Yuan Yao with Lü Bu's daughter. Both parties consented and Lü Bu's daughter left with Han Yin's escort. Chen Gui, the chancellor of Pei state and a former subordinate of Tao Qian, was recorded as not wanting the traitor Yuan Shu's power to continue to grow. Or, more realistically, his cousin Chen Yu had been defeated by Yuan Shu, so Chen Gui persuaded Lü Bu to break off his friendship with Yuan Shu and defect to Cao Cao. Lü Bu hurriedly caught up with the caravan, retrieved his daughter, arrested Han Yin, put him in shackles and sent him to Cao Cao, who executed Han Yin for treason.

In return, Cao Cao appointed Lü Bu as General of the Left. Cao Cao claimed that despite the poverty of his restored court, he had nonetheless donated his own wealth to forge the golden seal that Lü Bu deserved. Because the national treasury lacked ribbons, Cao Cao took off his own seal ribbon and gave it to Lü Bu (perhaps it was just a flattering statement). Cao Cao also claimed that Lü Bu's envoy had mistakenly lost his memorial accusing Yuan Shu of treason (which likely did not exist), and urged Lü Bu to resubmit it. Lü Bu, in turn, sent Chen Deng, son of Chen Gui, as an envoy to express his gratitude and request that Cao Cao officially appoint him as Governor of Xu Province and present Cao Cao with a high-quality ribbon. After meeting Cao Cao, Chen Deng declared that Lü Bu lacked strategy and sincerity and should be eliminated as soon as possible. Cao Cao then increased Chen Gui's salary and appointed Chen Deng as the prefect of Guangling, using him as a spy for Lü Bu's forces. Upon Chen Deng's return, Lü Bu learned that Cao Cao had only promoted Chen Gui and his son, without appointing Lü Bu as Governor. Furious, he threatened Chen Deng by striking a table with a sword. Chen Deng declared: "I said to Lord Cao, 'Treating General [Lü] is like raising a tiger. You need to keep it well fed, otherwise it will eat people.' Lord Cao instead said, 'That's not what you said, sir. You should treat it like a falcon, which will only serve you when it is hungry and will fly away when it is full.'" It is said that Lü Bu believed this statement and did not continue to blame Chen Deng.

The origin text of the Records of the Three Kingdoms suggests that Chen Deng completely deceived Lü Bu and prevented him from gaining any benefits from Cao Cao and emperor. However, the Biographies of Jiangbiao[Jiangbiao Zhuan, 江表傳] quoted an edict issued in the name of Emperor Xian to condemn Yuan Shu's claim to the throne, and the official title of the information provider, Lü Bu, was mentioned as "Governor of Xu Province".

The angered Yuan Shu ordered his top general Zhang Xun to lead a large army to attack Lü Bu. Previously, White Wave rebels Yang Feng and Han Xian had aided Emperor Xian in his exile, but were defeated by the schemes of Dong Cheng and Cao Cao, and now served as guest generals under Yuan Shu. Following Chen Gui's advice, Lü Bu sent a letter to Yang Feng and Han Xian, arguing that they were meritorious officials of the Han dynasty and should not serve the traitor Yuan Shu. Furthermore, if they defected, Yuan Shu's supplies would be divided among them. Lü Bu, along with Yang Feng and Han Xian, defeated Yuan Shu, killed ten of his generals, and plundered the empire before departing. Lü Bu wrote a letter humiliating Yuan Shu:

To save his reputation, Yuan Shu waited for Lü Bu to cross the river and retreat, then sent 5,000 men to review the troops on the other side of the river, but Lü Bu's soldiers saw this and laughed at them.

At this time, Xiao Jian, a minor warlord from the Langya state, in Xu Province, refused to submit to Lü Bu. Lü Bu wrote to Xiao Jian urging him to surrender, and Xiao Jian agreed and paid tribute to Lü Bu. However, Zang Ba, a rebel from Mount Tai, subsequently defeated Xiao Jian and seized his property. Furious, Lü Bu decided to personally attack Zang Ba. Gao Shun advised Lü Bu that failure would severely damage his reputation, but Lü Bu refused. Zang Ba defended the city, and Lü Bu was unable to capture it, so he reconciled with Zang Ba.

You, sir, arrogating your military might, often claim to have powerful generals and champions, hoping to destroy me, yet you always show mercy! Though I am weak, I was able to stride across Huainan like a tiger, while you, scurry to Shouchun like a rat, daring not to venture forth. Where are all those powerful generals and champions you speak of? You love to deceive the world with your boasts, but how can the world be deceived forever? Wars have always involved the exchange of envoys, and I was not the first to invent the strategy of sowing discord. If you are dissatisfied, you may continue to write to me.

You, sir, arrogating your military might, often claim to have powerful generals and champions, hoping to destroy me, yet you always show mercy! Though I am weak, I was able to stride across Huainan like a tiger, while you, scurry to Shouchun like a rat, daring not to venture forth. Where are all those powerful generals and champions you speak of? You love to deceive the world with your boasts, but how can the world be deceived forever? Wars have always involved the exchange of envoys, and I was not the first to invent the strategy of sowing discord. If you are dissatisfied, you may continue to write to me.

sections.heading: Fall and End
sections.content:
With Cao Cao's support, Liu Bei returned to reclaim his spot at Xiaopei in 197. When Yang Feng and Han Xian ran out of food, they requested to leave Lü Bu and go elsewhere to plunder grain. Lü Bu refused, instead ordering them to harvest Liu Bei's wheat. Yang Feng and Han Xian then conspired with Liu Bei to fight against Lü Bu. However, Liu Bei killed Yang Feng at a banquet, and Han Xian was killed by local officials while fleeing.

In 198, Liu Bei plundered Lü Bu's caravan on its way to buy horses from Zhang Yang. Lü Bu then renewed his alliance with Yuan Shu and sent Zhang Liao and Gao Shun to attack Xiaopei, defeating Liu Bei and his reinforcements led by Xiahou Dun. Liu Bei fled alone to Cao Cao, while his family was once again captured by Lü Bu.

Cao Cao followed with an invasion of Xu Province. Cao Cao sent a letter urging Lü Bu to surrender, and Lü Bu reportedly wanted to accept, but Chen Gong and others, out of hatred for Cao Cao, prevented their lord from doing so. Chen Deng, who had previously colluded with Cao Cao, seized the opportunity to launch a mutiny and lead Cao Cao's army. Lü Bu arrested Chen Deng's three brothers and threatened him, but Chen Deng remained unmoved, and Lü Bu's men secretly released them.

Lü Bu personally led his forces to confront Cao Cao, but was repulsed and retreated to the castle for defense. Seeing the tides turn against him, Lü Bu requested reinforcements from Yuan Shu. Yuan Shu resented Lü Bu for breaking their engagement, but Lü Bu's lobbyist argued, "Your Majesty[Yuan Shu], if you don't rescue Lü Bu, you'll be defeating yourself! If Lü Bu is defeated, Your Majesty will be defeated as well." Yuan Shu mobilized his troops to make a show of aiding Lü Bu, but he didn't engage Cao Cao directly. Lü Bu tied his daughter to his back and wanted to personally deliver her to Yuan Shu in exchange for substantial reinforcements, but he was unable to break through the encirclement of Cao Cao's archers and was forced to give up.

Lü Bu allegedly listened to his wife's advice and abandoned some reasonable methods of resistance. Two versions exist: Record of Heroes claims that Lü Bu intended to cut off Cao Cao's army's supply lines, but his wife pointed out the mutual hatred between Chen Gong and Gao Shun, and that Lü Bu's absence would make it impossible to suppress them. The Spring and Autumn of Wei House[Weishi Chunqiu] claims that Chen Gong asked Lü Bu to lead his troops outside while he defended the city and attacked the invading enemy. However, his wife claimed that Chen Gong was inherently treacherous, and Lü Bu's treatment of him was far inferior to that shown by Cao Cao.

Lü Bu entrusted his generals with their own actions, but their cliques hindered their cooperation, leading to their continued defeat by Cao Cao, who advanced all the way to Xiapi. Terrified, Lü Bu declared to Cao Cao's troops at the city gate, "Gentlemen, do not encircle me. I will personally surrender to our lord." Furious, Chen Gong berated Lü Bu, "What a damned lord, the traitor Cao Cao! Surrendering now is like trying to hit a rock with an egg. Do you expect to survive?"

Three months later, Lü Bu's troops were riddled with anxiety and impoverished by hunger. Cao Cao planned a water attack with Xun Yu and Guo Jia by using the two rivers, Yishui and Sishui. He initiated the attack in winter when the morale of Lu Bu's troops were at their lowest. The water attack was the breaking point for Hou Cheng, Song Xian, and Wei Xu. They captured Chen Gong, Gao Shun and surrendered to Cao Cao. Lü Bu led the last of his troops up to the city gates, and believing that there was little hope, he ordered his men to kill him and surrender to Cao Cao. His closest associates could not bear to do this, so Lü Bu went to surrender himself.

As he was brought before Cao Cao, Lü Bu allegedly offered some words to Cao Cao asking for his forgiveness. As was Lü Bu's previous practice, he used a kind of honorific that contained a sense of arrogance. The most famous version goes like this: "Milord, I am your only calamity. Now that I have surrendered, the whole world has nothing else to worry about. If you lead the infantry and I lead the cavalry, the world can be easily pacified." Upon hearing this, Cao Cao looked dismayed and considered the proposal. Upon noticing this, Liu Bei warned, "Lord, have you forgotten the fates of Ding Jianyang and Grand Master Dong?" Thusly convinced, Cao Cao ordered for his prisoner's execution. Before he was hanged, Lü Bu's last words to Cao Cao were, "Under any circumstances, you mustn't trust the one with large ears (Liu Bei)." Shortly after his death, Lü Bu's head was removed and his body was buried.

sections.heading: Personal Info
sections.content:
There are repeated accounts throughout historical records which attest to his strength. The historian Fan Ye, compiler for the Book of Later Han, compared Lü Bu to be the very thing which caused people to shirk with fear. There is a famous saying about him and Red Hare, "Among men Lü Bu, among horses Red Hare" (人中呂布, 馬中赤兔). According to the Chronicles of the Three Kingdoms, Lü Bu was a master in horse riding, archery, and armed combat. He was reportedly known as the "Flying General" (飛将).

However, strength alone was not enough to salvage his reputation. He was also known to be rough to the point of being nearly intolerable with other people, and he was known for his fickle nature. Chen Gong praised his lord as an invincible warrior in the field, but he also urged his lord to be cautious and to avoid disturbing the public opinion of him. Gao Shun claimed that Lü Bu was unable to appoint truly loyal men and did not plan carefully before doing anything, constantly blaming his wrong actions as "minor setbacks".

Historian Chen Shou remarked, "In history, there is no precedent in which a person like him was not ruined", as well as the fact that despite his strength Lü Bu lacked in proper battlefield strategy; he also only cared more about his gains. It was due to Lü Bu's aforementioned nature, he was known to be quite infamous for lack of control over his soldiers; not only did it make local populaces of the areas he conquered quite upset at him (due to the aforementioned soldiers committing unruly acts), but additional entries by Chen Shou had stated Lü Bu's soldiers and generals were all disunited due to their own motivations and Lu Bu's own lack of complete control and rationality towards them.

The factional struggle within Lu Bu's army is fully demonstrated in such a case: Hao Meng attempted an assault on Lü Bu whilst the latter was bare-naked (Lü Bu was with one of his women at this time). Surprised, the leading general fled from his office without even grabbing a bathrobe or any form of clothing as he fled in Gao Shun's camp. Lü Bu panicked, but Gao Shun calmly managed to find out the rebels' Henei accents and identified the mutineer is Hao Meng; Later, Cao Xing, a subordinate of Hao Meng, attempted to defect back to Lü Bu, but personally fought against Hao Meng, allowing Gao Shun to quell the mutiny. Cao Xing accused Chen Gong of being responsible for the mutiny to Lü Bu, but Lü Bu chose not to punish Chen Gong, but instead punished Gao Shun by sending his own trusted confidant Wei Xu to replace Gao Shun.

Despite Lü Bu's rather villainous historical record, during his active period, he could be seen as a sort of resistance power against the order of Yuan Shao (and perhaps Cao Cao). Emperor Xian sought his assistance after his escape from Li Jue. Zhang Chao's former subordinate, Zang Hong, considered Lü Bu's assassination one of Yuan Shao's three most treacherous acts of perfidy. Chen Shou dedicated a biography to Zang Hong, including a lengthy letter in which he denounced Yuan Shao, a rare occurrence for a vassal not directly under a major kingdom.

Many accounts suggest Lü Bu was a womanizer. Lü Bu's affair with Dong Zhuo's maid precipitated his patricide, a trait further romanticized in novels and folklore. In one account, Lü Bu, as a captive, complains that his subordinates betrayed his favor. Cao Cao sarcastically retorts that Lü Bu, unsatisfied with his existing lovers, is now having affairs with his subordinates' wives, leaving Lü Bu speechless. When Lü Bu's wife points out that he abandoned her during his defeat by Li Jue, Lü Bu's shame and unwillingness to betray her again may have hindered his ability to mount a more effective resistance against Cao Cao, a trait often mocked within the male chauvinistic moralizing of ancient China.

The historical record of Lü Bu's wife's surname is unknown, though the novel gives her surname as Yan. Historical records state that Wei Xu was Lü Bu's relative by marriage, but it is uncertain whether this refers to his wife or a concubine. He fathered one known daughter.

sections.heading: Romance of the Three Kingdoms
sections.content:
A painted mural of Lu Bu facing Liu Bei, Guan Yu, and Zhang Fei at Hulao Pass. Located at Long Corridor in the Summer Palace in Beijing, China

Lu Bu was said to be a master of all of the martial arts, a crack shot with the bow, and a peerless rider. His most major flaws were his impatience and his temper, and he preferred action to talk and diplomacy, and made a rather poor general and politician. The adopted son of Ding Yuan, he led the army that his stepfather brought to oppose the usurping Dong Zhuo. Before the negotiations even ended, Lu Bu got impatient and with a yell he charged alone into Dong Zhuo's front line, causing the panicked infantry to flee.

The fat tyrant realized that he would never defeat Ding Yuan so long as Lu Bu served him. Li Su, a general under Dong Zhuo and a fellow villager of Lu Bu, then volunteered to persuade the formidable warrior to defect. Bringing along a famous steed named the Red Hare, a thousand taels of gold, dozens of pearls and a jade belt (which would amount to a $1,000,000 bribe in today's market), Li Su came to see Lu Bu, who was encamped outside the city. It did not take much persuasion to convince Lu Bu to betray his master, who felt that his career was going nowhere under Ding Yuan. That very night, Lu Bu carried a sword into the tent of Ding Yuan, who was reading under the candlelight. Ding Yuan questioned him as to why he was holding the sword. Quickly sensing that Lu Bu had switched sides, Ding Yuan tried to escape but was unsuccessful.

With a stroke of his sword Lu Bu cut down his father's head, which he brought to Dong Zhuo the next morning. Lu Bu then took Dong Zhuo as his new adoptive father, who was overjoyed and showered his newly adopted son with more gifts. The constant presence of Lu Bu at his side deterred future assassins. For his personal safety, Dong Zhuo relied heavily on Lu Bu, who had by then been promoted to Knight General. The son would be seen beside the father almost all the time.

sections.heading: Battle with Three Heroes at Hulao Pass
sections.content:
Since he placed the puppet Emperor Xian in the throne, Dong Zhuo's tyrannical and cruel ways had angered many warlords around the country. The warlords formed a coalition under Yuan Shao in 190 and came for Dong Zhuo in the capital Luoyang to vanquish him.

However, they were stopped at Hulao Pass, 50 li from Luoyang. Riding forth on his Red Hare, his halberd in hand, dressed in the finest silver armor and wearing a twin pheasent-tail headpiece, Lu Bu taunted for challengers to duel him. Every warrior who came within range of his halberd were either maimed horribly or met a swift, bloody death. Amid the chaotic screams of panic and death, Lu Bu heard a loud, bass voice call him a "bastard slave with three last names". Wielding his Eight-Zhang Serpent Spear, Zhang Fei shouted the insult at Lu Bu to get his attention and galloped out to fight him.

Zhang Fei was said to have wielded the strength of a hundred men in battle, but he struggled against Lu Bu for more than fifty bouts, either side unable to gain an advantage. Then Guan Yu, brandishing his Green Dragon Crescent Blade, dashed out to assist his brother. The three fighters were engaged in another thirty bouts, but still Lu Bu held his ground. Then Liu Bei, holding up his Dual Swords, also joined the battle.

Like a merry-go-round, the three brothers galloped in a circle caging Lu Bu inside, who finally began to tire. Whenever Lu Bu tried to attack one, the other two would ride in and attack his exposed flanks forcing him back on the defensive, and soon he became exhausted. Unable to face the combined efforts of his three opponents at once, Lu Bu then made a feign at Liu Bei, whose martial ability he determined was the least of the three, and retreated through the resulting gap back to the pass gate. The Trio chased after Lu Bu but due to the speed of Red Hare, his horse, they could not keep up with him. However they abandoned the chase after they spotted Dong Zhuo and then pursued him unsuccessfully.

sections.heading: Romance with Diaochan and Slaying Dong Zhuo
sections.content:
An illustration of Lu Bu being chased away from Diaochan by Dong Zhuo.

After Dong Zhuo moved the capital to the more strategically sound Chang'an, Minister of Interior Wang Yun started to contemplate a plot to assassinate the tyrant by using Diaochan, a song girl who was brought up in his household but whom he treated like his own daughter, to plant the seed of dissension between Dong Zhuo and Lu Bu.

Inviting Lu Bu over one night, Wang Yun asked Diaochan to serve wine to the guest. Lu Bu was immediately left speechless by the girl's beauty. Well aware of this, Wang Yun then promised to marry Diaochan to the mighty warrior.

A few days later, however, Wang Yun laid a feast for Dong Zhuo and repeated the feat. Like Lu Bu, Dong Zhuo could not lift his eyes off Diaochan, who also displayed her prowess in song and dance. Dong Zhuo then brought Diaochan home and made her his concubine.

When Lu Bu heard about this the next morning, he headed for Dong Zhuo's bedroom and peeped in through the window. There he saw Diaochan sitting up grooming her hair while Dong Zhuo was still asleep. Seeing Lu Bu's reflection in the pond near the window, Diaochan then put up a sorrowful expression and pretended to wipe tears off her eyes with a handkerchief.

When Dong Zhuo fell sick a month later, Lu Bu took the chance to see Diaochan on the pretext of asking after Dong Zhuo's health, but this time Dong Zhuo woke up in time to see Lu Bu staring fixedly at Diaochan. The arrogant and ungrateful Dong Zhuo then had Lu Bu shoved out and forbidden to enter the inner chambers henceforth.

Then one day, while Dong Zhuo was holding a conversation with Emperor Xian, Lu Bu stole to his adoptive father's residence and met with Diaochan in the Fengyi Pavilion. Weeping, Diaochan pled with Lu Bu to rescue her from Dong Zhuo vowing to drown herself in the pond rather than spend another minute with the horrid tyrant. Taken in by Diaochan's act, Lu Bu placed his halberd aside and held Diaochan in his arms while comforting her with promises for her rescue.

Back at the palace, Dong Zhuo realized that Lu Bu had slipped away. Returning to his residence, he found the Red Hare horse outside and realized that Lu Bu had gone against his orders and reentered the inner chambers. In a huff, Dong Zhuo entered his residence to find the duo in the pavilion. The startled Lu Bu turned to flee, forgetting to retrieve his halberd in the process. Dong Zhuo grabbed the halberd and gave chase. Being too obese, Dong Zhuo could not catch up with the agile Lu Bu. He then hurled the halberd at Lu Bu but the latter fended it off and got away.

After the incident, Lu Bu was becoming increasingly fed up with Dong Zhuo's egotistical arrogance. This displeasure was further enticed by Wang Yun, who suggested subtly that Lu Bu take Dong Zhuo's life. Lu Bu attempted to argue for Dong Zhuo's paternal relationship to himself, but Wang Yun dismissed it, saying, "His surname is Dong and yours is Lu. Where were the paternal feelings when he threw that halberd at you?" Upon this, Lu Bu decided that he'd had enough and made up his mind to kill Dong Zhuo.

The conspirators then sent Li Su to fetch Dong Zhuo from his castle in the county of Mei under the pretense that the emperor intended to abdicate the throne to the warlord. Despite repeated ill omens, the overjoyed Dong Zhuo came to the palace gate, where his troops were barred from entering. As Dong Zhuo's carriage neared the palace building, soldiers loyal to Wang Yun surrounded the carriage and stabbed Dong Zhuo with spears.

Dong Zhuo was however injured only in the arms due to the breastplate he had taken the caution to wear that day. He then cried out for Lu Bu, giving him a "Imperial order to slay the traitor!". Unfortunately for Dong Zhuo, he was impaled in the throat by Lu Bu's halberd like a wild pig, while Lu Bu sarcastically proclaimed, "Then by imperial order I'll slay the traitor!" It was said that Lu Bu not only skewered his stepfather, but also the carriage as well, with the bloody tip of his halberd sticking out the back. Not content with this slaughter, Lu Bu called for the death of Li Ru as well.

sections.heading: Exile and Independence
sections.content:
After Dong Zhuo's death, rumors spread that the court intended to execute all his former troops from Liangzhou (涼州). When a royal decree of pardon was not issued, former subjects of Dong Zhuo, Li Jue and Guo Si, staged a coup and defeated Lu Bu within ten days. Escaping from Chang'an, Lu Bu first went to Yuan Shu in Yangzhou (楊州). Deterred by Lu Bu's fickleness, however, Yuan Shu declined to keep him.

Lu Bu then headed north to seek a position under Yuan Shao. Having been given some troops by the northern warlord, Lu Bu successfully flushed out the army under Zhang Yan. However, with his own force growing in strength, Lu Bu began to look like a threat to Yuan Shao. Sensing this himself, Lu Bu then bid his short-term master farewell. Yuan Shao sent assassins after Lu Bu, but Lu Bu eluded them.

In 194, while Cao Cao was away on a campaign against Tao Qian in Xuzhou (徐州), his subjects Zhang Miao and Chen Gong rebelled and handed Yanzhou (兗州) to Lu Bu. When Cao Cao heard the news, he quickly turned back and laid siege on Lu Bu in Puyang. After more than a hundred days of stalemate, a famine breakout forced Lu Bu to give up his position.

Thinking that forcing Cao Cao's retreat from Xuzhou had done Liu Bei a favor, Lu Bu then headed for Xiapi to take refuge under Liu Bei. He often referred to Liu Bei as "little brother" which Liu Bei's oath brothers, particularly Zhang Fei, found disrespectful. In 196, however, after a number of misunderstandings, Lu Bu turned on his host and took over Xiapi, proclaiming himself the governor and sending Liu Bei to the nearby town of Xiaopei. He later performs his historical act of preventing Ji Ling's attack on Liu Bei.

Hoping to prevent Cao Cao's expanding influence, Yuan Shu offered an alliance with Lu Bu. Their marriage alliance ends in the same manner with Lu Bu initially agreeing but then recalling his decision. Lu Bu also imprisoned Yuan Shu's envoy and sent the captive to Cao Cao as a token of friendship.

sections.heading: Downfall
sections.content:
In 198, Cao Cao and Liu Bei formed a short-term alliance and laid siege on Lu Bu in Xiapi. The siege dragged on for two months without significant gains. After the water attack on Xiapi flooded the city gates, Lu Bu's subjects rushed to inform their leader, but Lu Bu dismissed the threat on account that his Red Hare could run as well in water as on land. Thereupon he returned to his wine cups and consorts. However, heavy indulgence in alcohol and women had wasted his health away such that one day Lu Bu looked into the mirror and found himself looking thin and pallid. Making the resolution to quit drinking, Lu Bu then passed an order for all within the city to keep off wine.

One day, Hou Cheng caught a defector who attempted to steal fifty horses and give it to the enemy. Hou Cheng then prepared barrels of wine to celebrate with his colleagues. Fearing wrath of Lu Bu, he then had five bottles sent to his superior to appease the latter, which turned out to a terrible mistake. Seeing the bottles of wine, Lu Bu was enraged at the opposition to his alcoholic ban, and had Hou Cheng thrashed sixty times as punishment.

The unhappy Hou Cheng then plotted with two colleagues, Song Xian and Wei Xu, to betray Lu Bu to the enemy. Under the cover of the night Hou Cheng stole Lu Bu's Red Hare and galloped out of the only gate not submerged in water towards Cao Cao's camp.

The next morning, Cao Cao's troops launched a fierce attack on the city. Lu Bu personally took part in the defense of the walls. The battle dragged into high noon and the attackers backed off for a rest. The exhausted Lu Bu then slept in a guardhouse on top of the wall. Taking the opportunity, Song Xian and Wei Xu very carefully tied Lu Bu with chains and hoisted a white flag. The two also threw Lu Bu's halberd down the wall as proof. Seeing the signals, Cao Cao's troops then poured into the city and seized it in no time.

After he had been captured, he thrashed like a beast in his chains until he was taken in front of Cao Cao. Lu Bu attempted to appeal to Cao Cao in the same manner as historical counterpart. To reinforce his point, Lu Bu then tried to get Liu Bei to plea for him. Liu Bei continued to speak against Lu Bu by speaking the same words he did historically. Lu Bu's last words were spent threatening to kill Liu Bei like a dog for speaking against him. Cao Cao had Lu Bu strangled and then beheaded.

sections.heading: Trivia
sections.content:
- He is commonly known as "Roach" (小强—xiao qiang, lit. “little tough 'un", a slang name for a cockroach originated from Stephen Chow's films) in Chinese-speaking internet societies, due to the pheasant tails on his headpiece (resembling the antennae of the insect) and the fact of his apparent invulnerability in-game. The nickname "Cockroach" (ゴキブリ, Gokiburi or "G" for short) also appears in Japanese Dynasty Warriors fan communities for similar reasons. They even make jibs about Lu Bu "scuttling" across the field by noting the sound effect for insects walking (カサカサカサカサ, kasakasakasakasa).

- Lu Bu in his Dynasty Warriors 7 design appeared as a boss character during a collaboration quest in Road to Dragons.
